options.content:
# W&B API & GraphQL

Answers **"is the whole W&B app slow or erroring for this customer?"** Start here when a customer reports the UI is slow, requests fail, or the SDK can't reach the server.

This dashboard is about **overall API health** — request rate, error codes, latency, and whether the app is being throttled or is running out of capacity.

**To find out *which* GraphQL feature is failing** (launch, artifacts, entities, …) and drill to the actual failing request, open the [W&B GraphQL Errors](/d/wandb-graphql-errors) dashboard — error attribution lives there.

Use the **W&B Namespace** dropdown above to pick the customer's install.

panels.title: About: Request rate by status code

options.content:
**What it shows** — every HTTP request the W&B API serves, per second, split by response code. This is the single best "is the app up?" view.

**What bad looks like** — a wall of `5xx` appearing, or `2xx` dropping to near-zero (traffic no longer reaching the API: ingress / service / DNS / pod crash).

**Do next** — 5xx climbing → check pod logs and the **Saturation** section below. To attribute GraphQL errors to a feature, open [W&B GraphQL Errors](/d/wandb-graphql-errors).

**What it shows** — client errors (`4xx`) vs server errors (`5xx`) as two clean lines, so you can tell whose fault it is.

**What bad looks like** — any sustained `5xx` is a server problem. A `4xx` spike is usually the customer's SDK/auth (version mismatch, bad token, malformed query) and is often *not* an outage.

**Do next** — 5xx → check API pod logs and **Saturation** below. 4xx → confirm SDK version against **Service Image Versions** on [W&B Field Investigation](/d/wandb-field-investigation).

**What it shows** — the share of requests failing with a server error, as a percentage. Normalizes for traffic so a busy install and a quiet one read the same.

**What bad looks like** — above ~1% sustained is a real incident; a brief blip during a deploy is usually fine.

**Do next** — if the ratio is high, capture a snapshot of this panel and open [W&B GraphQL Errors](/d/wandb-graphql-errors) to see which feature is responsible, then drill to a trace.

**What it shows** — how long requests take, across all gorilla requests. p50 is the typical user; p95/p99 are the slowest 5% / 1%.

**What bad looks like** — p95 above ~3s sustained for more than a few minutes means users feel it. Brief spikes under load are tolerable.

**Do next** — if p95/p99 are high, use **p95 by operation** and **Top operations by slow request count** below to find the culprit, then Open Traces in Explore (top-right) and filter `duration>3s`.

**What it shows** — the 10 operations (trace span names) with the highest p95 latency, over time. Tells you *which operation* is slow, not just that something is. Sourced from spanmetrics (`traces_spanmetrics_*`, milliseconds) since gorilla's HTTP metrics don't carry a route label.

**What bad looks like** — one operation sitting well above the rest, especially filestream or graphql spans climbing after a deploy.

**Do next** — a slow ingest span (`.*[Ff]ile[Ss]tream.*`) → open [W&B Run Ingestion](/d/wandb-ingest). A slow graphql span → open [W&B GraphQL Errors](/d/wandb-graphql-errors). Then drill to a trace for that operation.

panels.title: Top operations by slow request count (>2.5s)
About: Top routes by slow request count

options.content:
**What it shows** — the routes that produced the most requests slower than 3 seconds over the selected range (counted from the latency histogram). This is the volume view of slowness: which operations hurt the most users.

**What bad looks like** — a route with a large, growing count of >3s requests, especially the workspace, run-table, or artifact routes.

**Do next** — note the top route, Open Traces in Explore (top-right), filter `service.name=gorilla` and `duration>3s` on that route to read a real slow trace.

panels.title: GraphQL operation rate by span (top, sampled)
panels.description: From spanmetrics (traces), which are sampled — trust the shape, not the absolute count. Spanmetrics have NO namespace label, so this is not filtered by $namespace; it reflects the whole trace stream.

options.content:
**What it shows** — the busiest GraphQL operations by request rate, from sampled traces. Good for seeing *what the customer's app is doing* and whether a particular operation surged.

**What bad looks like** — one operation dominating far above normal (a client hot-loop or retry storm), or the whole stream going flat (traffic stopped).

**Do next** — for *errors* by GraphQL service/operation, open [W&B GraphQL Errors](/d/wandb-graphql-errors). Note: spanmetrics have no namespace label, so this is not scoped to the selected install.

panels.title: GraphQL p95 latency by span (sampled)
panels.description: From spanmetrics (traces), which are sampled. Spanmetrics have NO namespace label, so this is not filtered by $namespace.

options.content:
**What it shows** — the slowest GraphQL operations at p95, in milliseconds, from sampled traces.

**What bad looks like** — an operation's p95 climbing into the seconds, especially if it lines up with the customer's complaint.

**Do next** — Open Traces in Explore (top-right), filter to that operation, and read the resolver chain. For error attribution by service, open [W&B GraphQL Errors](/d/wandb-graphql-errors).

panels.title: Rate-limited requests/sec
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). Counts requests the API throttled (429). Filtered by namespace="$namespace".

options.content:
**What it shows** — requests the API deliberately throttled (HTTP 429) because the client exceeded its rate limit.

**What bad looks like** — a high, sustained rate here **is not a fault** — throttling is the system protecting itself. It only matters if the customer complains their jobs are being slowed.

**Do next** — if throttling correlates with the customer's pain, it usually means their workload is too aggressive (too many parallel runs / too-frequent logging) or limits are set too low. This is a config conversation, not an outage. Compare with **Saturation** below to confirm the backend isn't actually overloaded.

panels.title: Admission rejections/sec
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). Admission control shedding load before it reaches the backend. Filtered by namespace="$namespace".

options.content:
**What it shows** — requests rejected by admission control (load shedding) before they reached the backend, including a cache-specific admission counter.

**What bad looks like** — a spike here means the API is protecting itself from overload — a symptom that the backend is near capacity. Like rate-limiting, it is a **defensive** signal, not a crash.

**Do next** — if this fires alongside high latency and rising **Saturation** gauges below, the install is genuinely overloaded → check backing-store health on [W&B Data-stores](/d/wandb-datastores) and container resources on [W&B Managed Install Performance](/d/wandb-managed-install-performance).

panels.title: Concurrency saturation (held / pending / active / capacity)
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). Concurrency-limiter gauges. Filtered by namespace="$namespace".

options.content:
**What it shows** — the API's concurrency limiter: `active`/`held` = work in flight, `pending` = requests queued waiting for a slot, `capacity` = the ceiling.

**What bad looks like** — `active` pinned at `capacity` with `pending` climbing = the API is saturated and queuing. This is the root cause behind high latency, 429s, and admission rejections.

**Do next** — if saturated, the backend can't keep up: check backing stores on [W&B Data-stores](/d/wandb-datastores) and pod CPU/memory on [W&B Managed Install Performance](/d/wandb-managed-install-performance). If everything downstream looks healthy, capture a snapshot and escalate.
